caption: Fig. 1. Log-log plots of weight gain vs time for alloys oxidized for 24 hr at 800°C: (a) alloys considered to form protective scales; (b) alloys which showed breakaway and healing kinetics.
paper_abstract: The isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity iron–manganese–aluminum alloys containing from 0 to 40% manganese and from 0 to 15% aluminum was investigated at 600, 800, and 1000°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for periods up to 100 hr. They were subsequently examined using SEM and metallographic techniques, and an oxide map showing the alloy structure and general oxidation behavior at 800°C was produced. Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. Alloys which contain more than 7.5% manganese form manganese rich scales, whereas alloys which contain lower levels of manganese form scales that are composed almost entirely of the oxides of iron. Small manganese oxide nodules grow through the alumina scales which form on alloys containing in excess of 9% aluminum. The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys

Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: The effect of manganese additions on the overall oxidation behavior of Fe–Mn–Al alloys is clearly seen in Fig. 2(a) where $\Delta W/24$ is plotted as a function of manganese concentration for alloys containing fixed levels of aluminum. For binary alloys which did not form continuous alumina scales, additions of less than about 2% manganese greatly increased the oxidation rate, whereas additions of between about 5 and 10% manganese significantly reduced it. Likewise, the effect of aluminum on the rate of oxidation of alloys containing fixed levels of manganese is shown in Fig. 2(b). Minima were observed for alloys which contained between about 6 and 9% aluminum. Subsequent SEM and metallographic examination of mounted and etched cross-sections revealed the structure of the alloys and four distinct morphological scale types. The “oxide map” $^{3}$ for the Fe–Mn–Al system as shown in Fig. 3 was constructed by superimposing the composition regions of each of the four morphological scale types upon the isothermal section of the phase diagram at 800°C.

Structurally, the major differences between the different nodule types were the presence of continuous layers of $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ and alumina or aluminate in small nodules, as opposed to the semi- or noncontinuous layers of both phases in the large nodules. The structures of the nodules observed at $800^{\circ}C$ are shown schematically in Fig. 17. The absence of $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ from the smaller nodules was probably due to the formation of a continuous layer of alumina or aluminate at the metal-oxide interface. Although no analyses were made, its greater thickness compared to that of alumina probably indicates an aluminate spinel. The presence of healing layers in small nodules and their absence in large ones can be explained if the growth rate of the large nodules was sufficiently high to prevent the formation of a continuous layer of aluminate. As the rate of oxidation of manganese is considerably lower than that of iron, $^{1}$ it is likely that formation of a continuous layer of $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ at the outer surface may retard the rate of ionic diffusion sufficiently to allow a healing layer of $Al_{2}O_{3}$ or aluminate to form at the base of the nodule and minimize further growth. The minima in Fig. 2(a) which occur at alloy compositions containing between 5% and 10% manganese are considered largely due to the elimination of large iron oxide nodules and their replacement with small manganese rich nodules and oxides. This transition was directly observed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al, which formed both large and small nodules.

The steady increase in 24 hr weight gains with increasing aluminum for alloys containing in excess of about 9% aluminum (Fig. 2b) is undoubtedly due to the increased number of such small manganese oxide nodules. If their growth is related to the formation of voids in the substrate, then void formation appears to be related to the concentration of aluminum in the alloy.

caption: Fig. 3. Oxide map for the Fe–Mn–Al system at $800^{\circ}$ C showing the alloy phase present and the composition regions with similar scale morphologies.
paper_abstract: The isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity iron–manganese–aluminum alloys containing from 0 to 40% manganese and from 0 to 15% aluminum was investigated at 600, 800, and 1000°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for periods up to 100 hr. They were subsequently examined using SEM and metallographic techniques, and an oxide map showing the alloy structure and general oxidation behavior at 800°C was produced. Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. Alloys which contain more than 7.5% manganese form manganese rich scales, whereas alloys which contain lower levels of manganese form scales that are composed almost entirely of the oxides of iron. Small manganese oxide nodules grow through the alumina scales which form on alloys containing in excess of 9% aluminum. The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: The effect of manganese additions on the overall oxidation behavior of Fe–Mn–Al alloys is clearly seen in Fig. 2(a) where $\Delta W/24$ is plotted as a function of manganese concentration for alloys containing fixed levels of aluminum. For binary alloys which did not form continuous alumina scales, additions of less than about 2% manganese greatly increased the oxidation rate, whereas additions of between about 5 and 10% manganese significantly reduced it. Likewise, the effect of aluminum on the rate of oxidation of alloys containing fixed levels of manganese is shown in Fig. 2(b). Minima were observed for alloys which contained between about 6 and 9% aluminum. Subsequent SEM and metallographic examination of mounted and etched cross-sections revealed the structure of the alloys and four distinct morphological scale types. The “oxide map” $^{3}$ for the Fe–Mn–Al system as shown in Fig. 3 was constructed by superimposing the composition regions of each of the four morphological scale types upon the isothermal section of the phase diagram at 800°C.

It was assumed that the alloy phases observed after 24 hr oxidation were similar in composition and proportion to the equilibrium phases and that the phase boundaries did not markedly shift with longer heat treatment times. On the basis of the small changes that were observed in the structures of the alloys Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al after 24 and 100 hr heat treatments and the similarity of the phase diagram determined by Schmatz $^{3}$ for 760°C, it is considered that the phase boundaries lie close to those at equilibrium. By superimposing the oxidation behavior of the ternary alloys upon the isothermal section of the phase diagram obtained in this way, the oxidation behavior at 800°C can be represented by way of an “oxide map” (Fig. 3) which shows alloy composition regions based on the observed scale morphologies.

Alloys in this region were mainly duplex, except for Fe–30Mn–9Al, which had a triplex $\alpha + \gamma + (\beta\mathrm{Mn})$ structure. At 800°C alloys from within the two phased region of the oxice map in Fig. 3 generally showed poor oxidation resistance, and the resulting scale morphologies were of two types. Alloys which contained relatively high proportions of ferrite formed alumina with bulky manganese rich oxides and oxide threads (Fig. 10a), which grew above grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces (Fig. 8a). Morphologies of this type were always directly related to the substrate structure and appeared as in Fig. 8(b). On alloys which contained higher proportions of austenite, the scale morphologies were generally bulky manganese rich oxides and wormlike oxide threads, which often appeared to have grown through the alumina (Fig. 10b). Morphologies of this type were also related to the structure of the substrate, and layers of ferrite which formed secondarily were frequently observed at the substrate surface as in Fig. 9.

From the work of Tomaszewicz and Wallwork, $^{11}$ manganese additions were apparently detrimental to the oxidation resistance of binary iron–aluminum alloys. The oxide map in Fig. 3 shows that all alloys in this system which contain less than 6% aluminum lack oxidation resistance and lie in regions I or II. For alloys which contained higher levels of aluminum, the addition of manganese was found to be beneficial, and alloys from region III were highly oxidation resistant. The optimum alloys were considered to contain between 5% and 10% manganese as this concentration resulted in the lowest weight gain for any group of alloys having the same aluminum concentration.

caption: Fig. 4. Cross-section of large oxide nodule formed on Fe-7.5Mn-3Al after 24 ml oxidation at 800°C.

The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: of the oxides of iron. A typical cross-section of a large oxide nodule formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al (Fig. 4) revealed a structure very similar to that of nodules formed on binary Fe–Al alloys. The nodule was composed mainly of a white outer layer ( $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ ) and an inner layer ( $Fe_{3}O_{4}$ ). X-ray images (Fig. 5) show a high degree of enrichment of manganese and aluminum in the $Fe_{3}O_{4}$ layer and at the gas-oxide interface. Manganese enrichment at the gas-oxide interface on alloys which contained more than about 5% manganese was found to correspond with an outer thin grey semicontinuous oxide layer. Both large and small nodules were formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al (fig. 6), and thus a transition from large to small nodules occurred at approximately 7.5% manganese. The structure of a similar typical small nodule (Fig. 7) consisted of a grey manganese oxide layer at the gas-oxide interface above a white layer ( $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ ). Particles of an aluminum rich phase were present in this layer and also formed a continuous layer at the nodule-alloy interface.

Aluminum rich phases, either $Al_{2}O_{3}$ or aluminate spinels, $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ , were also present either as particles or thin layers at the metal-oxide interface. A high degree of manganese enrichment on the surface of the external $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ layers on Fe–5Mn–6Al and Fe–7.5Mn–5Al and the low solubility of manganese oxides in $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ indicated the presence of an unknown phase. Closer examination of the scales revealed a grey phase $(\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3})$ present as particles at the gas-oxide interface and within the $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ layer on the Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy (Fig. 4).

citation_sentences: the surface (Fig. 8). Scales formed on alloys containing more austenite were often stratified and formed above ferritic surface layers which had become depleted in austenite (Fig. 9). A characteristic of alloys from region II was the growth of fine manganese oxide threads, which had a wormlike appearance (Fig. 10). Areas of spalled alumina were frequently associated with voids and bulky manganese rich oxides (Fig. 11). Structures of the scales were complex and frequently contained many phases as shown in Fig. 9. At relatively low concentrations of manganese in the alloy, the scales which formed had similar structures to those of the smaller oxide nodules from region I. As the alloy manganese concentration increased, the white phase $(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3)$ progressively disappeared and was no longer a scale constituent in alloys which contained more than $20\%$ manganese. X-ray images revealed high concentrations of manganese in scales formed on alloys in this region and the presence of aluminum rich phases at the alloy interface.

Alloys in this region were mainly duplex, except for Fe–30Mn–9Al, which had a triplex $\alpha + \gamma + (\beta\mathrm{Mn})$ structure. At 800°C alloys from within the two phased region of the oxice map in Fig. 3 generally showed poor oxidation resistance, and the resulting scale morphologies were of two types. Morphologies of this type were always directly related to the substrate structure and appeared as in Fig. 8(b). On alloys which contained higher proportions of austenite, the scale morphologies were generally bulky manganese rich oxides and wormlike oxide threads, which often appeared to have grown through the alumina (Fig. 10b). Morphologies of this type were also related to the structure of the substrate, and layers of ferrite which formed secondarily were frequently observed at the substrate surface as in Fig. 9.

As the alloy manganese concentration increased, the white phase $(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3)$ progressively disappeared and was no longer a scale constituent in alloys which contained more than $20\%$ manganese. X-ray images revealed high concentrations of manganese in scales formed on alloys in this region and the presence of aluminum rich phases at the alloy interface.

Alloys in this region were mainly duplex, except for Fe–30Mn–9Al, which had a triplex $\alpha + \gamma + (\beta\mathrm{Mn})$ structure. At 800°C alloys from within the two phased region of the oxice map in Fig. 3 generally showed poor oxidation resistance, and the resulting scale morphologies were of two types. Alloys which contained relatively high proportions of ferrite formed alumina with bulky manganese rich oxides and oxide threads (Fig. 10a), which grew above grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces (Fig. 8a). Morphologies of this type were always directly related to the substrate structure and appeared as in Fig. 8(b). On alloys which contained higher proportions of austenite, the scale morphologies were generally bulky manganese rich oxides and wormlike oxide threads, which often appeared to have grown through the alumina (Fig. 10b). Morphologies of this type were also related to the structure of the substrate, and layers of ferrite which formed secondarily were frequently observed at the substrate surface as in Fig. 9.

The alloy Fe–20Mn–6Al formed a ferrite surface layer during oxidation at $800^{\circ}$ C (Fig. 9) and oxidized rapidly with a weight gain after 20 hr in excess of 1 mg. The moderate oxidation resistance reported for austenitic alloys in the literature, $^{1}$ with weight gains typically of the order of 1 mg per day, may be attributed to the formation of bulky manganese rich scales and healing layers of aluminate or alumina beneath. The structures of the scales formed on austenitic alloys may thus be very similar to those of the bulky oxides described.

caption: Fig. 10. Manganese oxide threads formed on the surfaces of (a) Fe-20Mn-8Al and (b) Fe-30Mn-8Al oxidized for 24 hr at 800°C.
paper_abstract: The isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity iron–manganese–aluminum alloys containing from 0 to 40% manganese and from 0 to 15% aluminum was investigated at 600, 800, and 1000°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for periods up to 100 hr. They were subsequently examined using SEM and metallographic techniques, and an oxide map showing the alloy structure and general oxidation behavior at 800°C was produced. Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. Alloys which contain more than 7.5% manganese form manganese rich scales, whereas alloys which contain lower levels of manganese form scales that are composed almost entirely of the oxides of iron. Small manganese oxide nodules grow through the alumina scales which form on alloys containing in excess of 9% aluminum. The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.

Scales formed on alloys containing more austenite were often stratified and formed above ferritic surface layers which had become depleted in austenite (Fig. 9). A characteristic of alloys from region II was the growth of fine manganese oxide threads, which had a wormlike appearance (Fig. 10). Areas of spalled alumina were frequently associated with voids and bulky manganese rich oxides (Fig. 11). Structures of the scales were complex and frequently contained many phases as shown in Fig. 9. At relatively low concentrations of manganese in the alloy, the scales which formed had similar structures to those of the smaller oxide nodules from region I. As the alloy manganese concentration increased, the white phase $(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3)$ progressively disappeared and was no longer a scale constituent in alloys which contained more than $20\%$ manganese. X-ray images revealed high concentrations of manganese in scales formed on alloys in this region and the presence of aluminum rich phases at the alloy interface.

Alloys in this region were mainly duplex, except for Fe–30Mn–9Al, which had a triplex $\alpha + \gamma + (\beta\mathrm{Mn})$ structure. At 800°C alloys from within the two phased region of the oxice map in Fig. 3 generally showed poor oxidation resistance, and the resulting scale morphologies were of two types. Alloys which contained relatively high proportions of ferrite formed alumina with bulky manganese rich oxides and oxide threads (Fig. 10a), which grew above grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces (Fig. 8a). Morphologies of this type were always directly related to the substrate structure and appeared as in Fig. 8(b). On alloys which contained higher proportions of austenite, the scale morphologies were generally bulky manganese rich oxides and wormlike oxide threads, which often appeared to have grown through the alumina (Fig. 10b). Morphologies of this type were also related to the structure of the substrate, and layers of ferrite which formed secondarily were frequently observed at the substrate surface as in Fig. 9.

Wormlike threads of oxide which appeared to grow through alumina scales and on bulky oxides (Fig. 10) were associated with the presence of austenite beneath. Enrichment of aluminum in the outer layer of the bulky oxides was often observed and probably indicates that bulky oxide growth followed the formation of oxide threads. A schematic representation of the stages of growth considered to be involved is shown in Fig. 18.

Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. Alloys which contain more than 7.5% manganese form manganese rich scales, whereas alloys which contain lower levels of manganese form scales that are composed almost entirely of the oxides of iron. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: the surface (Fig. 8). Scales formed on alloys containing more austenite were often stratified and formed above ferritic surface layers which had become depleted in austenite (Fig. 9). A characteristic of alloys from region II was the growth of fine manganese oxide threads, which had a wormlike appearance (Fig. 10). Areas of spalled alumina were frequently associated with voids and bulky manganese rich oxides (Fig. 11). Structures of the scales were complex and frequently contained many phases as shown in Fig. 9. At relatively low concentrations of manganese in the alloy, the scales which formed had similar structures to those of the smaller oxide nodules from region I. As the alloy manganese concentration increased, the white phase $(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3)$ progressively disappeared and was no longer a scale constituent in alloys which contained more than $20\%$ manganese. X-ray images revealed high concentrations of manganese in scales formed on alloys in this region and the presence of aluminum rich phases at the alloy interface.

A characteristic feature of alloys which contained high levels of aluminum was the spallation which occurred on cooling. Examination of spalled areas revealed a high density of nodules and voids in the substrate. Most voids were smooth and rounded in contrast to voids formed on alloys from region II, which were mainly angular (Fig. 11a). Voids formed on all alloys which contained more than 25% manganese were smooth and rounded, regardless of the aluminum concentration in the alloy (Fig. 11b). Formation of similar smooth rounded voids beneath spalled $Cr_{2}O_{3}$ scales on a binary iron–chromium alloy $^{14}$ has been attributed to either evaporation or surface diffusion of chromium. Likewise, if diffusion of manganese vapor across voids does occur as proposed for a binary Fe–Mn alloy containing 40% Mn, $^{2}$ the growth of small manganese oxide nodules may be explained as follows.

Nodule nucleation occurs by reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor at flaws or defects in the alumina scale which lie directly above voids in the substrate. Nodule growth then proceeds almost exclusively by the reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor in the void. This is because the vapor pressure of manganese is many orders of magnitude larger than that of either iron or aluminum. Continued growth is mainly by the outward diffusion of $Mn^{2+}$ ions since $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ , $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ , and MnO are all p-type semiconductors. Inward growth of the nodule occurs by a mechanism involving short-circuit diffusion of oxygen, and “filling in” of the void with manganese oxide rather than iron oxide results. When the void has become filled with manganese oxide, rapid internal oxidation of aluminum in the substrate leads to the formation of a healing layer of $Al_{2}O_{3}$ or (Fe, Mn) $Al_{2}O_{4}$ at the nodule-alloy interface, and any further growth occurs at a much reduced rate via diffusion through this layer. The stages involved are shown schematically in Fig. 20. The observation of numerous smooth rounded voids “filled” or “partially filled” with manganese oxide, on the surface of Fe–30Mn–12Al (Fig. 11c), supports such a mechanism.

citation_sentences: Of the alloys oxidized for 24 hr only Fe–10Mn–4Al–12Cr, Fe–5Mn–5Al–5Cr, and Fe–7.5Mn–5Al–5Cr formed thin protective alumina scales. After 100 hr all alloys showed signs of scale degradation. With the exception of Fe–10Mn–4Al–12Cr, alloys which contained less than 5% aluminum formed hollow convoluted or wrinkled oxide mounds (Fig. 15a). Similar scale morphologies were observed on alloys which contained 5% aluminum and less than 5% chromium. Numerous very small manganese rich oxide nodules were observed on alloys which formed hollow oxide mounds. These can be seen in Fig. 15(b) in conjunction with manganese oxide threads which were observed on this alloy only. Where spallation occurred, the substrate contained voids, and remnants of the small oxide nodules appeared as internal oxides. Duplex alloys formed scales which were of two types. Scales formed on Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr appeared identical to those formed

Growth of manganese oxide threads on Fe–Mn–Al alloys was attributed to the presence of austenite below the alumina scale. Austenite was not present in the structure of Fe–7.5Mn–4Al–5Cr; however, oxide threads were present after 100 hr (Fig. 15b). Aluminum depletion of the substrate due to formation of an alumina scale may have been sufficient to form austenite, as the alloy composition was extremely close to the $\alpha-(\alpha+\gamma)$ phase boundary at 800°C.

citation_sentences: on certain alloys from region II of the oxide map, consisting of alumina and bulky oxides directly overlying precipitated austenite. Scales formed on Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr were extremely convoluted and appeared to have become detached from the substrate during oxidation (Fig. 16a). The substrate surface was totally ferritic and had become depleted of manganese during oxidation. Enrichment of aluminum occurred at the scale base, and iron and chromium concentrations were qualitatively similar to those in the alloy. Manganese concentrations were also similar to those of the alloy except for the thicker scale sections, which were highly enriched (Fig. 16b).

Breakaway during oxidation of the quaternary Fe–Mn–Al–Cr alloys can be directly attributed to the growth of hollow oxide mounds and convoluted scales. Comparison of the hollow oxide mounds with convoluted scales formed on the duplex alloy Fe–15Mn–5Al–5Cr revealed almost identical scale structures, except that the scale on the duplex alloy was more uneven in thickness (Fig. 16a). Bulky oxides formed above austenite plates on Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr were almost certainly highly enriched in manganese as were those formed on duplex ternary alloys. Formation of a totally ferritic substrate surface layer and areas of manganese enrichment in the scale of Fe–15Mn–5Al–5Cr indicates that bulky manganese oxides formed initially above precipitated austenite. The compositions and structures of the convoluted scales were not determined. However, their greater thickness than alumina scales and their high degree of enrichment in aluminum suggests that they were composed of the higher oxides of iron, manganese, and chromium with alumina or aluminate spinel at the scale base.

caption: Fig. 17. Structures of nodules formed on Fe–Mn–Al alloys at $800^{\circ}$ C. Type 1: large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys containing less than about 5% manganese. Type 2: large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys containing between about 5% and 7.5% manganese. Type 3: small manganese rich nodules formed on alloys which contained at least 7.5% manganese.

citation_sentences: Structurally, the major differences between the different nodule types were the presence of continuous layers of $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ and alumina or aluminate in small nodules, as opposed to the semi- or noncontinuous layers of both phases in the large nodules. The structures of the nodules observed at $800^{\circ}C$ are shown schematically in Fig. 17. The absence of $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ from the smaller nodules was probably due to the formation of a continuous layer of alumina or aluminate at the metal-oxide interface. Although no analyses were made, its greater thickness compared to that of alumina probably indicates an aluminate spinel. The presence of healing layers in small nodules and their absence in large ones can be explained if the growth rate of the large nodules was sufficiently high to prevent the formation of a continuous layer of aluminate. As the rate of oxidation of manganese is considerably lower than that of iron, $^{1}$ it is likely that formation of a continuous layer of $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ at the outer surface may retard the rate of ionic diffusion sufficiently to allow a healing layer of $Al_{2}O_{3}$ or aluminate to form at the base of the nodule and minimize further growth. The minima in Fig. 2(a) which occur at alloy compositions containing between 5% and 10% manganese are considered largely due to the elimination of large iron oxide nodules and their replacement with small manganese rich nodules and oxides. This transition was directly observed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al, which formed both large and small nodules.

caption: Fig. 18. Stages of growth of bulky manganese rich oxides during oxidation of duplex $\alpha + \gamma$ Fe-Mn-Al alloys at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $\mathrm{PO}_2 = 200$ torr). Stage 1: formation of alumina scale over an initially ferrite matrix. Stage 2: nucleation and growth of austenite plates beneath the alumina scale. Stage 3: Growth of $\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ oxide threads at weaknesses in the overlying alumina scale. Stage 4: incorporation of the oxide threads and alumina scale into the growing bulky manganese rich oxide, involving manganese depletion and conversion of the austenite back to ferrite. Stage 5: formation of a continuous healing layer of $\mathrm{Al}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ or (Fe, Mn)AlO.

paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: The structures of the bulky oxides that formed on alloys in this region appeared to depend upon the concentration of manganese rather than aluminum in the alloy. Alloys which contained less than about 20% manganese had very similar structures to the small bulky oxide nodules which formed on alloys containing at least 7.5% Mn from region I of the oxide map. Alloys with more than approximately 20% manganese formed bulky oxides highly enriched in manganese; these alloys contained no $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ , and the phases appeared similar to the manganese rich phases reported to form on a binary Fe-40Mn alloy. $^{2}$ Typical structures of such oxides are vastly different from those of scales formed on binary iron-aluminum alloys. They are composed predominantly of manganese oxides; however, their modes of growth appear generally similar to those of nodules on the binary alloys. The different structures observed on Fe-25Mn-7Al (Fig. 19a) are considered to contain the phases $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ , $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ , $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ , and either $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ or $Al_{2}O_{3}$ , and are shown schematically in Fig. 19(b). Whether formation of the phases $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ and $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ does occur appears to depend on the enrichment of manganese and the growth of $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ at the metal-oxide interface.

Compositional differences between $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ and $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ as indicated by iron and manganese X-ray images were not observed, and because $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ may contain in excess of 90 mol% $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ ,¹ conversion to the tetragonal $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ phase, $(\mathrm{Fe},\mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ , may occur with only minor enrichment of manganese. Thus $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ was present only in some structures which contained $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ ; for example, the oxides numbered 2, 3, and 6 in Fig. 19(a). Where both $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ and $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})_{3}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ were absent from the scales, for example, the oxides numbered 1 and 7 in Fig. 19(a), layers considered to be $(\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}$ or $Al_{2}O_{3}$ were present at the metal-oxide interface. When complete aluminate layers were not observed, phases of the type $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ were present, for example, in the oxides numbered 2–6 in Fig. 19(a).

caption: Fig. 20. Stages of growth of small manganese oxide nodules during oxidation of $\alpha$ and $\alpha + \beta$ -Mn Fe-Mn-Al alloys at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $\mathrm{PO}_2 = 200$ torr). Stage 1: void formation beneath alumina scales. Stage 2: flaw or defect formation in the overlying alumina. Stage 3: outward growth of $\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ . Stage 4: "Filling in" of the void via short-circuit diffusion of oxygen. Stage 5: Formation and growth of $\mathrm{Al}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ or (Fe, Mn) $\mathrm{Al}_2\mathrm{O}_4$ particles at the oxide-metal interface of the filled-in void. Stage 6: Formation of a healing layer and conversion of the nodule to $\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ .
paper_abstract: The isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity iron–manganese–aluminum alloys containing from 0 to 40% manganese and from 0 to 15% aluminum was investigated at 600, 800, and 1000°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for periods up to 100 hr. They were subsequently examined using SEM and metallographic techniques, and an oxide map showing the alloy structure and general oxidation behavior at 800°C was produced. Scales formed on alloys which contain insufficient aluminum to form protective alumina have structures which depend largely upon the concentration of manganese in the alloy. Alloys which contain more than 7.5% manganese form manganese rich scales, whereas alloys which contain lower levels of manganese form scales that are composed almost entirely of the oxides of iron. Small manganese oxide nodules grow through the alumina scales which form on alloys containing in excess of 9% aluminum. The most oxidation resistant alloys, having compositions within the range Fe–(5–10)% Mn–(6–10)% Al, develop continuous protective alumina scales and are totally ferritic. Austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex alloys as it promotes the breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and the growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
paper_title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
citation_sentences: Nodule nucleation occurs by reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor at flaws or defects in the alumina scale which lie directly above voids in the substrate. Nodule growth then proceeds almost exclusively by the reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor in the void. This is because the vapor pressure of manganese is many orders of magnitude larger than that of either iron or aluminum. Continued growth is mainly by the outward diffusion of $Mn^{2+}$ ions since $Mn_{2}O_{3}$ , $Mn_{3}O_{4}$ , and MnO are all p-type semiconductors. Inward growth of the nodule occurs by a mechanism involving short-circuit diffusion of oxygen, and “filling in” of the void with manganese oxide rather than iron oxide results. When the void has become filled with manganese oxide, rapid internal oxidation of aluminum in the substrate leads to the formation of a healing layer of $Al_{2}O_{3}$ or (Fe, Mn) $Al_{2}O_{4}$ at the nodule-alloy interface, and any further growth occurs at a much reduced rate via diffusion through this layer. The stages involved are shown schematically in Fig. 20. The observation of numerous smooth rounded voids “filled” or “partially filled” with manganese oxide, on the surface of Fe–30Mn–12Al (Fig. 11c), supports such a mechanism.
